explanation.text: Tom remembers Chancey as a sleek, well-presented horse but now sees his shabby winter coat. That contrast shows that “well turned-out” refers to a neat appearance, which the shabby coat lacks.

Paragraph 3 presents Chancey on arrival with a shabby, foamy winter coat, while paragraph 11 recalls his glossy coat and rippling muscles the previous season. The paragraphs therefore compare his appearance at two times.
Chancey settles, gently searches Tom's pockets, accepts a mint, and later calmly eats while Tom leaves. These behaviors reveal that beneath his nervous arrival he can be relaxed and friendly.

Tom insists clipping will make Chancey look fine, and Nick calmly says the neglected coat will not be much of a problem. Both boys therefore express confidence that effort and care can help the horse.
Tom obeys the required lunch visit mainly to stop his mother from complaining about how much time he spends at Sandy Lane. This reason reveals that being at the stable matters greatly to him.

Chancey first appears shabby, frantic, and difficult, disappointing Tom. Once treated gently, however, he settles, nuzzles Tom, and accepts food, therefore supporting the theme that a hard situation may be better than it appears.

Tom approaches slowly, speaks gently, offers a mint, and brushes Chancey; the horse then settles and nuzzles him instead of jumping nervously. This response connects the horse's calm mood to Tom's reassuring behavior.

Paragraph 2 shows birds nesting on skyscrapers and bridges, butterflies migrating through streets, and bats living in a church belfry. These examples explain how species use built city spaces as habitat.

The article lists the essentials wild animals need—food, water, hunting or hiding space, and places to raise young—then states that a city supplies all of them. This directly explains why wildlife survives there.
Biologists and officials want wildlife to remain healthy and encounter humans safely in shared city spaces. In this context, “interacts” means comes close to or has contact with.

Because ants remove discarded food, much as earthworms turn scraps into useful soil and bats consume huge numbers of insects, their helpful cleanup and control roles make all three good additions to a city.

The author calls cities natural habitat, gives examples of many urban species, and urges readers to look for wildlife in any big city. These points support treating both urban and rural spaces as wildlife homes.
The narrator reports Larry's private thoughts as he lies awake comparing kinds of fishing and decides to become a lobsterman. Access to those thoughts shows how important lobster fishing is to Larry.

Paragraph 1 locates Larry in a Maine harbor filled with fishing boats, while paragraph 5 expands the coast to beaches, rocky islands, and seals. Together, the descriptions build a broader understanding of the seaside setting.
Paragraph 6 explains that lobsters hide in cool water among seaweed and rocks. The thick kelp in the cove matches that habitat, so Larry has a clear reason to expect lobsters there.
After a trap sinks and disappears among the kelp, its brightly striped buoy remains floating on the surface to mark the spot. Therefore, the buoy helps fishers locate and retrieve their trap.

Larry and his father repeatedly return for more traps and work all day dropping them one by one before going home. That sustained effort best supports a theme that hard work is necessary for a successful catch.

Larry wants his family to eat the first three lobsters caught in his own trap, then lies awake planning to become a lobsterman. His wish to celebrate and continue the work shows pride in his new ability.

Setting traps in many locations, making repeated trips for more, and returning to shore after a full day describe the central work Larry does with his father. Therefore, those actions matter more to a summary than clothing or scenery.
